events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario describes hot work on a residential job where the "permit scope exceeded" - meaning the work performed went beyond what was authorized under the hot work permit. This is a non-compliance issue.

Key facts:
- Hot work, Safety & Rigging scope
- Observed field condition: permit scope exceeded
- Stage: rough-complete
- References: NFPA 51B, OSHA 1926 Subpart J

NFPA 51B requires hot work permits to define the scope and duration of work. Exceeding the permit scope is a violation. OSHA 1926 Subpart J covers welding and cutting safety.

Decision: fail - permit scope exceeded is non-compliant
Risk: high - hot work outside permitted scope is a serious safety hazard
Component conditions: non-compliant
Percent complete: rough-complete, maybe 70%
Confidence: high, 0.9

  "findings": [
    "Hot work performed exceeds the scope authorized under the issued hot work permit",
    "Work outside permit scope means fire watch, area clearance, and combustible control measures may not cover the actual work performed"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Stop all hot work immediately",
    "Re-evaluate the job scope and either amend/issue a new hot work permit covering the full scope or remove the out-of-scope work",
    "Verify fire watch coverage, combustible clearance, and atmospheric conditions match the actual work area before resuming",
    "Inspect the out-of-scope work area for fire hazards or damage before closing in"
  ],
  "rationale": "NFPA 51B requires a hot work permit that defines the location, scope, and duration of hot work. Exceeding the permit scope is a direct non-compliance because the safety controls (fire watch, combustible removal, area isolation) were planned only for the permitted scope. OSHA 1926 Subpart J reinforces fire prevention and protection requirements for welding/cutting. Work must fail until the permit is corrected and controls are verified for the actual scope.",
  "references": [
    "NFPA 51B — Standard for Fire Prevention During Welding, Cutting, and Other Hot Work",
    "OSHA 1926 Subpart J — Welding and Cutting"
  ]
}
